summary: I was living in Chicago at 64th and California when I entered the service in August 1944. Growing up on the south side of Chicago shaped much of my early life, including attending Lindblom High School. Chicago was home during my formative years and the place where my family lived while I prepared to join the military.

I graduated from Lindblom High School in Chicago in 1944. While I was there, they had an ROTC program, which I joined with the hope of becoming a commissioned officer. Lindblom was where I finished my formal education before entering the Navy.

At Lindblom High School, I was part of the ROTC program, aiming to become a commissioned officer. I believe I reached the rank of first lieutenant in the program. The ROTC experience prepared me somewhat for military life and leadership.

I remember hearing about the attack on Pearl Harbor on December 7, 1941, while at a temple bazaar with my parents. It was a pivotal moment that changed everything, and people thought the war would be over quickly. Pearl Harbor was the event that really brought the war home to us.
cues: start of US involvement in WWII

summary: The attack on Pearl Harbor happened when I was 13 years old, in 1941. I was at a temple bazaar with my parents when we heard the news. Many people thought the war would be over in a week, but it wasn’t, and that attack marked the beginning of a long conflict.

I joined the Boy Scouts when I was 12, although the troop I joined folded after about six months. Still, I was interested enough to continue on a provisional basis and eventually earned my Eagle Scout. Scouting taught me discipline and skills that helped later in the military.

Alan Milach was a friend of mine who was ahead of me in school and qualified for the Army A-12 military program. He urged me to join the Navy before I was drafted, advice that ultimately saved me from being in a program that was later discontinued. We kept in touch by mail while he was at Hunter College in New York City.

Alan Milach, a friend of mine, was sent to Hunter College in New York City as part of the Army A-12 program. He wrote to me from there, encouraging me to join the Navy before being drafted. Hunter College was part of the military educational programs early in the war.

The DVD companion to the film 'Patton' mentioned how early commissioning educational programs like the Army A-12 were discontinued at General Patton’s request to meet troop needs. This was relevant because the program my friend Alan was in got washed out due to that decision. The film helped me understand the military context of those changes.

I enlisted in the United States Navy in August 1944, despite having asthma as a child, which I did not disclose. The Navy took me through boot camp and service school at Great Lakes, and I trained as a Quartermaster. Serving in the Navy was my way to contribute to the war effort in the Pacific.

I went through boot camp and service school at the Great Lakes Naval Training Station near Chicago. There, I had a one-on-one interview to determine my Navy rating and was assigned Quartermaster as my second choice. Great Lakes was also where I recovered from scarlet fever during an epidemic.

I trained and served as a Quartermaster in the Navy, which meant working on the bridge of the ship handling navigation and keeping the ship's log. It was a very academic role, requiring daily records of weather and ship conditions. I was proud to have this position rather than a storekeeper role.

Iwo Jima was one of the major battles in the Pacific that my fellow servicemen talked about when I was in boot camp. The thought of carrying blood on an invasion like Iwo Jima was daunting, and it made me consider the realities of war. Although I was trained as a Quartermaster, I was aware of the intense fighting happening there.

Like Iwo Jima, the Battle of Okinawa was a significant and brutal campaign during the war. I heard about it while training, and it underscored the dangers faced by Navy personnel in the Pacific. These battles shaped the context of my service even though I was not directly involved in frontline combat.

After completing training at Great Lakes, I traveled by troop ship to San Francisco, which was a major embarkation point for Navy personnel heading to the Pacific. From there, I began the process of finding my assigned ship and moving further into the war zone. San Francisco was a key transit point in my naval journey.

During my transit to the Pacific, we stopped at Pearl Harbor, which was still a major naval base after the 1941 attack. It was a significant landmark on the way to the combat zones, and I remember hearing about V-E Day while there. Pearl Harbor remained central to naval operations throughout the war.

I initially thought my ship might be at Samar in the Philippines, but when I arrived there, it was not. Samar was one of the stops on my journey through the Pacific, but I had to continue on to Leyte and beyond to find my ship. It was part of the complex logistics of moving personnel in the war.

Leyte was another stop on my way to join my ship, after Samar. From Leyte, I traveled through the Admiralty Islands and Hollandia before finally locating the USS APC-4. Leyte was a key location in the Philippines during the war and part of my journey in the Pacific.

The Admiralty Islands were part of the route I took traveling from Leyte to Hollandia in New Guinea. These islands were strategic during the war and part of the chain of bases supporting operations in the Pacific. My journey through these islands was part of the complex naval movements in 1945.

Hollandia in New Guinea was where I finally spotted my ship, the USS APC-4. I got off the ship I was on to check with the port office and confirmed my ship was heading to the Philippines. Hollandia was a major base and a turning point in my deployment.

The USS APC-4 was my assigned ship, a small wooden personnel transport about 103 feet long, built in Maine. I joined it at Hollandia and later traveled with it to the Philippines, including Subic Bay. Serving on the APC-4 was a unique experience given its size and role in the Pacific.

I served in the Philippines aboard the USS APC-4, docking at places like Subic Bay. The Philippines were a major theater of operations in 1945, and I experienced the naval life there, including rare trips into town. It was an important part of my service in the Pacific.

Subic Bay was the naval base in the Philippines where my ship was stationed. While tied up there, we rarely got off the ship, but on one occasion, a few of us went into town for ice cream. Subic Bay was a large and important base during the war.

While en route to the Pacific, we heard news of V-E Day, marking the end of the war in Europe. This was a significant morale boost as it meant the focus was now fully on the Pacific Theater. The timing of V-E Day was during my transit to join my ship in 1945.

New Guinea was a key location in my journey to the Philippines, with Hollandia being a major base there. The island was strategically important in the Pacific campaign, and I passed through it as part of my naval deployment in 1945. It was part of the chain of islands supporting operations against Japan.

To reach my ship in the Philippines, I was flown as the only passenger on a DC-3 aircraft. This was part of the effort to get me to Subic Bay quickly after I spotted my ship leaving Hollandia. The DC-3 was a reliable transport plane used extensively during the war.

While serving in the Pacific, I kept in touch with my family using V-mail, the wartime precursor to email. I even included sketches in my letters to convey things that might not be transmitted otherwise. V-mail was a vital link to home during my deployment.

entity: Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki
summary: Though I was not directly involved, the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki were pivotal events that ended the war in the Pacific. These bombings occurred while I was serving in the Navy in 1945. They marked the conclusion of the conflict I was part of.

During my naval service, I underwent the traditional Shellback initiation when crossing the equator. It was more of a lighthearted ceremony than harsh hazing, and I have the certificate framed as a keepsake. This rite of passage was part of naval tradition and camaraderie.
